text: {"matches":[{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":114,"rationale":"Both state that eggshell color does not affect nutritional value."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":440,"rationale":"Both claim brown eggs are not more nutritious than white eggs, i.e., shell color has no nutritional impact."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":12,"rationale":"Claim 2 refers to an extra‑large egg, a specific size, while claim 12 gives protein for a generic egg."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":10,"rationale":"Claim 2 gives calories for an extra‑large egg; claim 10 gives calories for a generic egg."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":54,"toIndex":12,"rationale":"Claim 54 specifies a large boiled egg’s protein content, a specific case of the generic protein amount in claim 12."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":58,"toIndex":14,"rationale":"Claim 58 focuses only on choline, a subset of the nutrients listed in claim 14."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":58,"toIndex":51,"rationale":"Claim 58 mentions choline only, whereas claim 51 lists choline among several key nutrients."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":57,"toIndex":84,"rationale":"Both advise consulting a doctor before increasing egg intake for people with high cholesterol."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":70,"toIndex":12,"rationale":"Claim 70 specifies a large egg’s protein amount, a specific case of the generic protein amount in claim 12."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":71,"toIndex":259,"rationale":"Both state that eggs contain all essential amino acids, making them a high‑quality protein source."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":74,"toIndex":73,"rationale":"Claim 74 provides a satiety‑index measurement, a specific evidence supporting the general statement in claim 73."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":63,"toIndex":62,"rationale":"Claim 63 reports increased blood lutein/zeaxanthin after egg consumption, a specific outcome of the lutein content described in claim 62."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":66,"toIndex":64,"rationale":"Claim 66 reports a study showing triglyceride reduction from omega‑3‑enriched eggs, a specific instance of the broader claim 64."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":15,"toIndex":82,"rationale":"Claim 15 reports that egg cholesterol does not negatively affect health, a specific finding within the broader mixed evidence of claim 82."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":201,"rationale":"Both state that increased egg consumption is not linked to cardiovascular disease risk."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":276,"rationale":"Both conclude that egg consumption shows no significant effect on health outcomes."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":158,"rationale":"Claim 156 says no association with CVD risk, while claim 158 says eating four eggs per week raises CVD risk."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":272,"rationale":"Claim 156 reports no CVD association, whereas claim 272 reports a decreased CVD risk, implying an effect."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":277,"rationale":"Claim 156 says no CVD link, claim 277 reports a 7% increased CVD mortality per egg per day."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":156,"toIndex":279,"rationale":"Claim 156 finds no CVD association, claim 279 states each extra egg per day raises CVD risk by 4%."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":171,"toIndex":170,"rationale":"Claim 171 details bans in specific countries, a subset of the general practice described in claim 170."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":14,"toIndex":215,"rationale":"Claim 14 lists a few vitamins and choline, which are included within the broader nutrient list of claim 215."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":230,"toIndex":247,"rationale":"Claim 230 notes egg yolk as a natural source of vitamin D, a specific source within the broader claim 247 that eggs are high‑vitamin‑D foods."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":249,"toIndex":247,"rationale":"Claim 249 specifies an intake range (7–14 eggs/week) to mitigate seasonal vitamin D drop, a specific application of the general claim 247."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":341,"toIndex":370,"rationale":"Claim 341 gives quantitative GHG emissions for eggs, a specific metric supporting the broader statement in claim 370 that eggs have lower environmental impact."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":341,"toIndex":340,"rationale":"Claim 341 quantifies eggs’ lower GHG contribution, a specific evidence for the broader claim 340 that eggs have lower impact than other animal proteins."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":382,"toIndex":383,"rationale":"Both discuss egg‑related mortality risk but focus on different outcomes (all‑cause/CVD mortality vs CHD and stroke)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":389,"toIndex":390,"rationale":"Claim 389 examines gut‑microbiota effects of egg intake versus choline supplements, while claim 390 reports TMAO rise only with choline supplements; both address choline but different endpoints."}]}
